name: Matthew 18 conflict resolution
summary: When wronged by a brother, escalate one step at a time. Private first; witnesses next; the assembly last. The goal is restoration, not vindication.

steps.title: Go privately
steps.do: Go to them alone and tell them their fault — between you and them only. The first conversation is private.

steps.title: Take two or three witnesses
steps.do: If they will not hear, take one or two others along so that every word may be established by the mouth of two or three witnesses.

steps.title: Tell the assembly
steps.do: If they refuse to hear the witnesses, bring the matter to the church. If they refuse even the church, let them be as a Gentile and a tax collector.

failure_modes: Public broadcast before private conversation — skipping step 1
Going alone past step 2 — single-witness escalation
Framing the goal as vindication rather than restoration
Ending at step 1 when the offense is unrepented and a brother stays bound

name: Discernment — wait, ask, walk
summary: For a hard decision: don't lean on your own understanding. Ask. Wait. Walk only when the next step is clear.

steps.title: Acknowledge you don't know
steps.do: Lean not on your own understanding. The starting point is that your reasoning alone is insufficient.

steps.title: Ask for wisdom
steps.do: If any of you lacks wisdom, let them ask of God, who gives generously and without reproach. Pray for wisdom directly.

steps.title: Wait
steps.do: Wait on the LORD. Be still. Wait patiently. Rushing past the wait is the most common failure mode.

steps.title: Listen for the small voice
steps.do: Your ears shall hear a word behind you saying, This is the way, walk in it — when you turn to the right or to the left.

steps.title: Walk when the next step is clear
steps.do: In all your ways acknowledge Him, and He shall direct your paths. Take the step that is in front of you; the next will be given when needed.

failure_modes: Skipping the wait — making the call from your own reasoning under time pressure
Asking for wisdom but not stopping to listen
Demanding to see the whole path before taking the next step
Treating doors that open as automatic confirmation

name: Confession — owning a fault
summary: When you've done wrong: name it before God; if it affects others, name it to them. Forgiveness flows on the back of confession, not before it.

steps.title: Stop covering
steps.do: He who covers his sins will not prosper. The first move is to stop hiding what you've done — from yourself first.

steps.title: Confess to God
steps.do: If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. God receives confession directly.

steps.title: Confess to those affected
steps.do: Confess your faults one to another and pray for one another that you may be healed. If your sin wronged another, the confession must travel where the wound traveled.

steps.title: Make restitution where you can
steps.do: Zacchaeus's pattern: 'If I have taken anything from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold.' Restitution where possible; restoration in any case.

failure_modes: Confessing to God only when others were wronged
Public spectacle confession to performative ends
Confession without ceasing the behavior
Demanding instant forgiveness before the wronged party has received the confession

name: The four-gate witness — RED → FLOOR → BROTHERS → GOD
summary: To make a claim that stands: the claim must be true (RED), grounded in principle (FLOOR), corroborated by witnesses (BROTHERS), and submitted to God (GOD). The engine's own framework. Walk it.

steps.title: RED — Is the claim itself true?
steps.scripture: John 14:6 — I am the way, the truth, and the life
steps.do: Run the claim against the engine. If the math closes, RED passes. If it doesn't, the claim falls here and goes no further.

steps.title: FLOOR — What principle does it stand on?
steps.do: Even a true claim has to stand on something. Identify the principle. If you can't name the FLOOR, the claim is unanchored.

steps.title: BROTHERS — Who confirms?
steps.do: In the mouth of two or three witnesses every word is established. Who else has tested this and found it sound? Without witnesses, the claim is solo and waits.

steps.title: GOD — Have you waited?
steps.do: Wait on the LORD. Acting before God has confirmed is the most common failure. If you cannot wait, you do not yet trust.

failure_modes: Skipping RED — moving forward on what you didn't verify
Skipping FLOOR — true claim but no principle anchoring it
Skipping BROTHERS — solo decisions called 'inspired'
Skipping GOD — using urgency to justify acting before peace comes

name: Test the spirits — discerning what is from God
summary: When a teaching, prophecy, leader, or movement arrives. Don't accept it on the basis of its claim. Test it.

steps.title: Beloved, believe not every spirit
steps.do: Skepticism is the starting posture, not unbelief. Many false prophets are gone out into the world. You are commanded to test, not to receive uncritically.

steps.title: Does it confess Christ as come in the flesh?
steps.do: Every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God; every spirit that does not is not of God. Doctrine of Christ is the first test.

steps.title: Does it match the fruit?
steps.do: You shall know them by their fruits. A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit. Watch what grows under their teaching over time.

steps.title: Does it align with Scripture?
steps.do: Though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you than that which we have preached, let them be accursed. Scripture is the floor; even a sign-working teacher who contradicts the Word fails the test.

steps.title: Prove all things; hold fast that which is good
steps.do: Test thoroughly. Keep what stands. Discard what doesn't. Both halves matter.

failure_modes: Accepting authority because of signs without doctrinal test
Accepting because the messenger is charismatic
Refusing to test because 'judge not' was misread
Throwing out the whole teaching because of one wrong note (failing 'hold fast that which is good')

name: Receiving reproof
summary: When someone tells you you're wrong. The wise man's response is opposite of the fool's. Faithful are the wounds of a friend.

steps.title: Receive before defending
steps.do: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful. The first move is to receive — even if the messenger is imperfect — not to defend.

steps.title: Test which kind of reprover
steps.do: Rebuke a wise man and he will love thee. Brothers who restore are gentle. Reprovers who delight in your fall are different. Distinguish — but receive both kinds for what they show.

steps.title: If wise, learn; if foolish, weigh
steps.do: The ear that heareth the reproof of life abideth among the wise. He that refuseth instruction despiseth his own soul. The cost of refusal is high.

steps.title: Repent or correct as appropriate
steps.do: He who covers his sins will not prosper, but he who confesses and forsakes them will have mercy. If the reproof landed because it was true, change. If untrue, set it aside without bitterness.

failure_modes: Counter-attacking the reprover instead of weighing the reproof
Hearing only the tone, missing the content
Receiving without changing — soft yes, hard heart
Letting the same correction land repeatedly without change
